aliases: Braised Chicken and Pork Rice Set for Two

english: Braised Chicken and Pork Rice Set for Two

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a multi-component Chinese rice set meal; mapped to the broader 'rice dish' class (FOODON_00002473) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (rice-based composite dish) used as the closest defensible category for a multi-component Chinese rice set meal with braised chicken, spicy pork, and pickled radish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for a multi-component Chinese rice set meal; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One 2-pax rice set typically comprises two portions of steamed white rice (~150 g each), one braised chicken dish (~150 g), one hot-and-spicy pork slices dish (~150 g), pickled radish (~50 g), and two beverages, totalling approximately 800 g of solid food per set.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — 双人米饭套餐 2-Pax Rice Set (G00010501)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical record G00010501 for the 2-Pax Rice Set. All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported in the HPB record are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; white rice (boiled, average)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite 2-pax rice set. GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component — boiled white rice — as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). The composite set includes braised chicken, spicy pork, and pickled radish, which contribute protein, fat, and fibre that will modestly attenuate the effective GI in practice. GL per serving calculated as: GI 72 × available carbohydrate per serving (50.4 g for ~200 g rice portion) / 100 = 36.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g; a full 800 g set delivers approximately 1,848 mg sodium, approaching the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced sauce and seasoning across all dishes in the set, avoid adding soy sauce or chili sauce at the table, and note that the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (327 mg K vs 231 mg Na per 100 g) partially offsets the sodium load but does not eliminate the risk at full-set portion sizes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice (GI ~72) with 25.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g; dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100 g provides partial glycaemic buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit rice to one small portion (~100–150 g cooked), request brown rice or mixed-grain rice if available, prioritise the protein-rich braised chicken and pork components, and avoid sweetened beverages included in the set to minimise postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 4.21 g/100 g with saturated fat composition unknown; sodium 231 mg/100 g; cholesterol reported as 0 mg/100 g in HPB source.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should be cautious of the unknown saturated fat content from braised chicken and pork preparations, request steaming or minimal-oil cooking methods, limit portion size to reduce cumulative sodium and fat intake, and choose water or unsweetened beverages over sweetened drinks included in the set.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Set includes pork (red meat) and likely chili-based sauces; both are associated with pro-inflammatory dietary patterns relevant to RA.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should verify whether the pork component is included and consider substituting with chicken or tofu, avoid chili and tomato-based condiments that may trigger flares, and request minimal-oil preparation to reduce saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The 2-Pax Rice Set (双人米饭套餐) is a Chinese-style shared set meal commonly served in Singapore's casual dining and food court establishments. It comprises two portions of steamed white rice, a braised chicken dish (黄焖鸡), a hot-and-spicy boiled pork slices dish (水煮肉片), pickled radish (爽口脆萝卜), and two beverages. The set is designed for two diners sharing communal dishes, a format typical of Chinese family-style dining. Nutritionally, the set provides 164 kcal per 100 g, with 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate. Dietary fibre is notably present at 3.7 g/100 g, contributed by the pickled radish and vegetable components. Sodium is moderate at 231 mg/100 g but accumulates significantly at full-set portion sizes (~800 g), delivering approximately 1,848 mg sodium per set. The dominant carbohydrate source is white rice, which carries a high glycaemic index (~72), making portion control important for glycaemic management. The braised chicken and spicy pork components contribute protein and fat, with saturated fat composition not separately reported in the HPB source. The pickled radish adds crunch, acidity, and a modest fibre contribution. This dish is suitable for general consumption but requires portion awareness for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. It is a popular lunch and dinner option in Singapore's Chinese food court and zi char restaurant segment.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical record G00010501 with confidence 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed with provenance canonical. All core macros are present and internally consistent. GI is estimated from the dominant white rice component using the University of Sydney GI Database, which is an approved GI source.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Chinese rice set meal is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo record G00010501 as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB source for this record. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g in the HPB source (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo G00010501); this is plausible for a composite dish where the HPB analytical method may not have detected measurable cholesterol at the per-100g level across the full set average, though individual components (chicken, pork) would be expected to contain cholesterol — consumers should be aware this may reflect a composite averaging effect. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on boiled white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008). GL per serving calculated using approximately 200 g cooked rice per person (50.4 g available carbohydrate) × GI 72 / 100 = 36.3. The beverage component of the set is unspecified; if sweetened beverages are included, the effective carbohydrate and sugar load will be higher than the per-100g values suggest. Halal status is unconfirmed as the set includes pork.
